data.title: Is It Time To Revisit Daiichi Sankyo (TSE:4568) After Its Recent Share Price Weakness?
data.description:
• Investors may be wondering whether Daiichi Sankyo Company stock still offers value after recent volatility, or if the current price already reflects its prospects.
  • The stock last closed at ¥2,700.5, with a 7 day return of 1.9%, a 30 day return of 1.9%, declines of 23.5% year to date and 27.9% over the past year, and a 5 year gain of 18.3%.
  • These mixed returns have put valuation firmly in focus for investors who are considering how much risk is being priced into the stock and whether the recent weakness points to changing sentiment. Recent coverage has centered on how the company is positioned within the pharmaceuticals sector and whether its current pricing still lines up with its fundamentals.
  • Daiichi Sankyo Company records a value score of 4 out of 6. This suggests several checks flag the stock as potentially undervalued. The sections ahead will compare different valuation approaches before finishing with a broader way to think about what that valuation may mean for you.

Find out why Daiichi Sankyo Company's -27.9% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Daiichi Sankyo Company Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a stock could be worth by projecting the cash it may generate in the future and then discounting those amounts back to today using a required rate of return.

For Daiichi Sankyo Company, the 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model starts from the latest reported free cash flow, which shows a loss of ¥96,269.68m. Analysts have provided explicit free cash flow estimates out to 2031, with Simply Wall St extending the projections further using its own assumptions. For example, projected free cash flow in 2031 is ¥336,482.80m. The ten year path between 2026 and 2035 is based on a mix of analyst inputs and extrapolated estimates.

Discounting these future cash flows back to today produces an estimated intrinsic value of ¥4,626.24 per share. Compared with the recent share price of ¥2,700.5, the model implies the stock trades at a discount of about 41.6%, which indicates that, on this DCF view, Daiichi Sankyo Company may be undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Daiichi Sankyo Company is undervalued by 41.6%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 15 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Daiichi Sankyo Company.

Approach 2: Daiichi Sankyo Company Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each unit of current earnings, which makes it a common anchor when you compare stocks within the same sector.

A higher or lower P/E often reflects what the market is pricing in for earnings growth and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually supports a lower P/E as a more normal or fair level.

Daiichi Sankyo Company trades on a P/E of 18.91x. That sits above the Pharmaceuticals industry average of 16.26x, but below the peer group average of 21.26x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” for the stock of 32.22x, which is the P/E level suggested by factors such as its earnings growth outlook, profit margins, industry, market value and risk profile.

This Fair Ratio approach can be more useful than a simple comparison with peers or the industry, because it adjusts for the company’s own growth expectations, risks, margins, sector and size. On this basis, Daiichi Sankyo Company’s current P/E is below the Fair Ratio, which indicates that the stock may be trading at a discount on earnings.

Result: UNDERVALUED

data.title: Valuetronics Holdings (SGX:BN2) One Off HK$37.8M Loss Tests Bullish Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Advertisement

Valuetronics Holdings (SGX:BN2) FY 2026 earnings snapshot

Valuetronics Holdings (SGX:BN2) has reported FY 2026 first half revenue of HK$836.6 million and basic EPS of HK$0.23, setting the tone for a set of results that put earnings quality and margins in focus. Over recent periods the company has seen revenue move from HK$862.1 million in FY 2025 H1 to HK$866.9 million in FY 2025 H2 and then to HK$836.6 million in FY 2026 H1. Basic EPS tracked at HK$0.22, HK$0.19 and HK$0.23 across those same halves, giving investors a clearer view of how profitability is being earned rather than just reported.

See our full analysis for Valuetronics Holdings.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set these results against the main narratives around growth, risks and profitability to see which stories hold up and which need to be reconsidered.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Margins under pressure at 7.1%

  • Over the last 12 months, Valuetronics recorded a net profit margin of 7.1%, compared with 9.9% in the prior year, while trailing 12 month revenue sat at HK$1,660.2 million and net income at HK$117.1 million.
  • Critics highlight that the weaker 7.1% margin supports a bearish focus on profitability pressure, yet the period also includes a HK$37.8 million one off loss that complicates the picture:
    • The move from 9.9% to 7.1% margin lines up with the cautious view that an electronics manufacturer can face cost or pricing pressure even when revenue, at HK$1,660.2 million for the latest trailing 12 months, is broadly stable compared with HK$1,729.1 million a year earlier.
    • At the same time, the HK$37.8 million one off loss means part of the margin decline is not from day to day operations, so a strictly bearish take that treats all of the margin drop as structural does not fully match the data.

HK$37.8m one off loss distorts earnings

  • The trailing 12 month net income of HK$117.1 million reflects the impact of a single HK$37.8 million one off loss that weighed on reported profit more than regular operations alone would suggest.
  • What stands out for a bullish reading is that this one off event heavily shapes the last year’s earnings, while the core profit trend is steadier than the headline suggests:
    • Across the last three reported half years, net income excluding extra items moved between HK$79.9 million and HK$92.9 million, with FY 2026 H1 at HK$93.0 million on revenue of HK$836.6 million, which indicates that underlying half yearly profitability remained within a relatively tight band despite the separate one off charge in the trailing data.
    • Forecast earnings growth of 13.8% per year is framed against this base where the one off HK$37.8 million loss has already been absorbed, so bullish investors argue that cleaner future periods, without such items, could look different from the recent 7.1% margin snapshot.

Bulls who think the one off HK$37.8 million loss is masking the underlying story may want to see how that view stacks up against a full narrative and valuation breakdown for Valuetronics, then compare it with how other investors are weighing margin pressure against earnings growth forecasts. Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

P/E of 26.9x with DCF fair value gap

  • Valuetronics trades on a trailing P/E of 26.9x at a share price of S$1.17, compared with an Asian Electronic industry average of 35.8x, a peer average of 16.5x, and a DCF fair value of S$0.74.
  • Valuation focused bears point to this data as a challenge to a simple “cheap stock” story, because the signals pull in different directions:
    • Being on 26.9x P/E while peers average 16.5x supports the cautious view that, relative to closer comparables, the stock carries a richer multiple even though it is below the broader Asian Electronic industry’s 35.8x.
    • The current price of S$1.17 sitting above the DCF fair value of S$0.74 further backs a bearish tilt that questions how much of the forecast 13.8% annual earnings growth is already reflected in today’s valuation.

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a stock could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those cash flows back to today using a required rate of return.

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to connect what you pay for the stock with the earnings the business is currently generating. It gives you a quick sense of how many dollars investors are willing to pay today for each dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E often reflects growth expectations and risk. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while lower growth or higher risk usually points to a lower multiple being more reasonable.

data.title: Agilent Technologies (A) Margin Gains Reinforce Bullish Recurring Revenue Narrative After Q2 2026 Results
data.description:
Agilent Technologies (A) has just reported Q2 2026 results with revenue of US$1,835 million and basic EPS of US$1.20, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of US$7.2 billion and EPS of US$5.00 that sit against a one year earnings growth figure of 21.3%. Over recent quarters, revenue has ranged between US$1,668 million and US$1,861 million while quarterly EPS has moved between roughly US$0.75 and US$1.53, with net profit margin across the last year at 19.6% compared to 17.6% a year earlier. This sets up a story focused on how durable that margin profile looks from here.

See our full analysis for Agilent Technologies.

Advertisement

  • Over the last 12 months, earnings grew 21.3%, compared with a 3.3% average annual growth rate over five years, and trailing 12 month EPS sits at US$5.00 on about US$7.2b of revenue.
  • Analysts' consensus view links this step up in profit growth to stronger demand in end markets like pharma and advanced materials. The more modest 3.3% five year average reminds you that recent earnings strength is much faster than the longer run trend, which could make future results more sensitive if those demand drivers cool.

Net margin at 19.6% supports bullish case

  • Net profit margin over the last year was 19.6%, compared with 17.6% a year earlier, on trailing 12 month net income of about US$1.4b from US$7.2b of revenue.
  • Consensus narrative argues that cost savings from the Ignite operating model and a higher mix of recurring items like consumables, software, and services can support stronger profitability. The move from 17.6% to 19.6% margins aligns with that bullish view, although tariff related cost pressures highlighted in the risks section show that efficiency gains and higher margin mix are having to work against rising input costs at the same time.
    • The recurring revenue focus in areas such as CrossLab and services fits with the higher trailing margin, which can help smooth earnings across cycles, yet the need to offset tariff driven costs means investors still need to watch whether margins stay near 19.6% if those headwinds persist.
    • Improved margin combined with trailing 12 month EPS of roughly US$5.00 gives bulls a profitability base to reference when thinking about the forecasts for earnings to grow around 12.9% per year, even though that forecast growth is slower than the 21.3% achieved in the last year.

  • Bears highlight that forecast revenue growth of about 6.4% per year and earnings growth of roughly 12.9% per year both sit below the cited broader US market growth rates. This slower expected growth helps explain why the stock trades at a discount to the DCF fair value and analyst target, even though the P/E is lower than peers, because some investors may see the growth profile as less compelling versus higher growth alternatives.
    • The combination of a 27x P/E and a current price below both the US$161.31 target and US$173.41 DCF fair value leaves room for different views on whether the discount reflects caution about growth or a potential opportunity if the forecasts play out as expected.
    • With earnings up 21.3% over the last year, the slower forecast rates versus the broader US market give skeptics a concrete reason to question whether that recent pace is sustainable, which directly feeds into how much weight to put on the valuation gap.
Skeptics think the lower growth forecasts justify a cheaper P/E, but the current discount to both DCF fair value and analyst target means the valuation debate is far from settled 🐻 Agilent Technologies Bear Case
